publications.editedComment: This tutorial preserves two presentation modes without splitting its content model. Channel data supplies the preferred tutorial configuration and hero image, with legacy cards used only as fallback. Inline mode is a normal document section. Modal mode adds a labeled dialog, close button, backdrop dismissal, Escape handling, numbered sections, and a stored per-channel auto-show preference. Opening the modal locks body scrolling; close and unmount restore it, and the global key listener is removed. Sections marked under construction remain visible but are labeled and visually muted. Reduced-motion preferences disable the entrance transition. The butterfly guides; the tutorial store decides when guidance should appear.

This page keeps its ink on the surface. A fixed hero introduces Mermaids of Venice, followed by the book cover, a short premise, an external paperback link, and explicit notices that the PDF and signed copies are not presently available through the giftshop. The personal note is visibly a placeholder reserved for Silas's own words. A separate paragraph discloses that the book itself was not AI-written while identifying that disclosure as the page's artificial exception. The final gallery strip draws from the named art collection. No store, chat, generator, or model call enters the study; this is a static human-facing book landing page.

Implementation report: `mermaids-page` is intentionally static. The template renders a fixed hero image, cover image, descriptive copy, an Amazon link opened with `noopener`, two availability notices, a human-note placeholder, an AI disclosure, and `ProjectGalleryStrip` for the `mermaids-of-venice` collection. Its script declares no reactive state, store access, API request, chat surface, or generation action. The comments explicitly preserve the page as a human-authored front end. Future work is content replacement and giftshop availability, not hidden runtime behavior. The Component presents publication context accurately and avoids implying that the book or page is generated on demand.

The sketch is labeled before anyone mistakes it for the finished machine. This page reserves a Portos Server Setup tab and describes a future flow: accept a server address, validate the endpoint, and save the user/server pairing through normal server records. It also states, in the rendered interface, that the flow is not built yet and that the placeholder exists so the Conductor tab split can land without inventing a persistence contract. There are no inputs, submit controls, validators, requests, or stores in this file. The frantic lines are roadmap copy only. A useful empty frame, but still an empty frame.

Portos is a reserved docking bay, not a connected server console. The Component renders a title, one paragraph explaining the intended user/server pairing, a Planned flow card, and a Not built yet card. Its proposed orbit is clear: collect a Porto address, verify that endpoint, and persist metadata through the shared server records rather than local Component state. None of those operations exists here today—there is no form, reactive value, health request, save call, or store import. The page earns credit for marking the vacuum honestly. Future explorers know where the controls belong without being told that the ship has already launched.

Wishmaster treats a wish as an existing Dream shape rather than opening a second archive. Computed lists filter the Dream store to `dreamType === 'WISH'`, then divide active and inactive records and expose the first eight as recent wishes. Submission requires a title, uses the pitch or title as descriptive text, and creates a private, non-mature, active Dream with human creation source and no project field supplied. Success clears the form; failure preserves a message. Mount loads Dreams with inactive records included when necessary. The page can create and summarize wishes, but it does not edit, delete, attach projects, or provide the larger project infrastructure described by contrast.

Well, sprout, this cauldron uses the Dream store you already own! Active, inactive, total, and recent wish lists all come from records whose `dreamType` is WISH. A new request needs a title; an empty pitch borrows that title instead of boiling nothing. `createDream` receives a private, active, human-created wish with a wand icon and no project attachment. A good save empties both fields, while a bad one leaves an error message where you can see it. Mount fetches up to 200 Dreams including inactive ones when the cupboard is empty. It makes wishes, not miracles: no editing, deletion, or project conversion lives here.

Punch up the label: this is a create-and-stream utility, not a full Chat Manager. The form collects one message and a numeric recipient id, disables submission while loading or incomplete, then calls `chatStore.addChat` with the current user, optional current Bot, `ToBot` type, and no Character. It awaits `streamResponse` for the new chat id before appending the original new-chat record to its local list. Existing conversations are never fetched, and the streamed reply is not separately added here. Errors go only to the console. Finally, both inputs clear whether the operation succeeded or failed. Functional prototype, misleadingly broad heading.

This utility cleans one narrow hallway. It accepts message text and a recipient id, creates a `ToBot` chat through the store, requests a streamed response, and displays only chats created during the current Component session. The sender comes from `userStore`; Bot identity is the current Bot when available. The local array is not synchronized with the broader chat history, and no initial load occurs. Submission errors are logged but not rendered to the visitor. The `finally` block resets the form and loading state even after failure. Useful for exercising the create path, but it should not be mistaken for the production conversation browser its heading suggests.
